walkthrough.title: Organize the Spice Cubes
walkthrough.text: Let's get Century: Spice Road set up. First, find the four types of spice cubes. These are small wooden cubes in four colors. Yellow cubes are turmeric, the most common spice. Red cubes are saffron. Green cubes are cardamom. And brown cubes are cinnamon, the rarest and most valuable. Sort them into four piles in the middle of the table where everyone can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Point Cards
walkthrough.text: Now find the point cards. These are the cards with victory point values and spice cost icons printed on them. Give them a good shuffle and place them in a face-down deck. Then deal five point cards face up in a row from left to right. This is the point card market. Players will claim these during the game by paying spices.

walkthrough.title: Place the Coins
walkthrough.text: Find the gold and silver coin tokens. Place the gold coins on top of the leftmost point card in the market row, and the silver coins on top of the second point card. When someone claims one of those cards, they also take the coin sitting on it. Gold coins are worth three victory points and silver coins are worth one. After those cards are claimed, new cards slide left and fresh coins go on the new first and second positions.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Merchant Cards
walkthrough.text: Now find the merchant card deck. These cards have spice icons and arrows showing what they do. Give them a good shuffle and deal six merchant cards face up in a row from left to right. This is the merchant card market. Place the remaining merchant cards in a face-down deck next to the row.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Merchant Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player receives two starting merchant cards that are the same for everyone. The first is a production card that generates two yellow cubes. The second is an upgrade card that lets you upgrade two cubes, moving them up the spice ladder. These go directly into each player's hand. They are not part of the merchant market.

walkthrough.title: Give Out Starting Spices
walkthrough.text: Now let's hand out starting spices based on turn order. The first player gets three yellow cubes. The second player gets four yellow cubes. The third player gets four yellow cubes. The fourth player gets three yellow cubes and one red cube. If you have a fifth player, they get three yellow cubes and one red cube. Each player places their starting spices in front of them. This is your caravan.

walkthrough.title: Set the Caravan Limit
walkthrough.text: One important rule before we start: each player can hold a maximum of ten spice cubes at any time. This is your caravan limit. If you ever gain spices that would put you over ten, you must discard cubes of your choice down to ten. Keep an eye on your count throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a random player to go first. Play proceeds clockwise from the first player. Everyone should be able to see both market rows clearly. You are all set to start trading spices and building your merchant empire. Let's learn the rules.

bullets: Sort cubes into 4 supply piles by color
Value order: yellow (turmeric) → red (saffron) → green (cardamom) → brown (cinnamon)

bullets: Shuffle point cards into a face-down deck
Deal 5 face-up in a row (left to right) as the point card market

bullets: Gold coins (3 VP each) on the 1st (leftmost) point card

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure
walkthrough.text: Century: Spice Road is played in turns going clockwise around the table. On your turn, you must perform exactly one of four possible actions. You can play a merchant card from your hand, acquire a new merchant card from the market, claim a point card by paying spices, or rest to pick up all your played merchant cards. Let's go through each action in detail.

walkthrough.title: Action: Play a Merchant Card
walkthrough.text: Choose one merchant card from your hand and place it face up in front of you. Then carry out that card's effect immediately. There are three types of merchant cards. Production cards show spice cubes with no arrows. They give you those exact cubes from the supply. Trade cards show cubes with arrows pointing to other cubes. They let you exchange spices according to the conversion shown. Upgrade cards let you move cubes up the spice ladder.

walkthrough.title: Merchant Card Types: Production
walkthrough.text: Production cards are the simplest. They show one or more spice cube icons with no arrows. When you play one, take the shown cubes from the supply and add them to your caravan. For example, a card showing three yellow cubes gives you three yellow cubes. Remember, you cannot exceed ten total cubes in your caravan.

walkthrough.title: Merchant Card Types: Trade
walkthrough.text: Trade cards show an input on the left, an arrow, and an output on the right. Pay the input cubes back to the supply and take the output cubes. Here is the key: trade cards are repeatable. You can execute the same conversion as many times as you want in a single play, as long as you have the input cubes. For example, if a card says two yellow converts to one red, and you have six yellow cubes, you could do that trade three times in one action to get three red cubes.

walkthrough.title: Merchant Card Types: Upgrade
walkthrough.text: Upgrade cards let you move cubes up the spice value ladder. The ladder goes yellow to red to green to brown. Each upgrade moves one cube up exactly one step. So a yellow becomes a red, a red becomes a green, or a green becomes a brown. The number on the upgrade card tells you how many upgrades you get to perform. You can spread these upgrades across different cubes or stack them on the same cube.

walkthrough.title: Action: Acquire a Merchant Card
walkthrough.text: Instead of playing a card, you can pick up a new merchant card from the market row. The leftmost card in the row is free. Every card to the right of it costs one spice cube per card you skip. Place one cube from your caravan on each card you pass over, then take the card you want into your hand. Those cubes you placed stay on the skipped cards, making them more attractive to future players.

walkthrough.title: Acquiring Cards: Picking Up Bonus Cubes
walkthrough.text: When you acquire a merchant card that has spice cubes sitting on it from other players' acquisitions, you take all those cubes and add them to your caravan. This is a nice bonus. Sometimes the deeper cards in the row are worth the cost because they have accumulated several free cubes. After you take a card, slide the remaining cards to the left to fill the gap, and deal a new card from the deck to the rightmost position.

walkthrough.title: Action: Claim a Point Card
walkthrough.text: When you have the right combination of spices, you can claim one of the five face-up point cards. Return the exact spice cubes shown on the card to the supply, then take the card and place it face down in your scoring pile. If that card had a gold or silver coin on it, take the coin too. Slide remaining point cards left to fill the gap and deal a new one to the rightmost position. Place coins on the new first and second positions if they do not already have them.

walkthrough.title: Action: Rest
walkthrough.text: When you rest, pick up all the merchant cards you have previously played and return them to your hand. That is your entire turn. You do not get to play a card or do anything else. Resting is necessary because once you play a merchant card, it stays on the table until you rest. Managing when to rest is a key part of the game's tempo.

walkthrough.title: The Spice Ladder
walkthrough.text: Yellow turmeric is the lowest value. Red saffron is the next step up. Green cardamom is the third tier. And brown cinnamon is the most valuable. Point cards that require higher-value spices tend to be worth more victory points. Your goal is to efficiently convert common spices into rare ones to claim high-value cards.

walkthrough.title: The Coin Race
walkthrough.text: Remember those gold and silver coins from setup? They create a race element in the game. Gold coins sit on the leftmost point card and are worth three extra victory points. Silver coins sit on the second card and are worth one extra point. Whenever a card in those positions is claimed, the coins go with it. When the row refills, new coins go on the new first and second cards. Getting coins is a significant scoring boost, so there is real incentive to claim cards quickly rather than always waiting for the perfect moment.

walkthrough.title: End of Game Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game end is triggered when any player claims their fifth point card if you are playing with four or five players, or their sixth point card if playing with two or three players. When this happens, finish the current round so that every player has had the same number of turns. Then move to final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Add up your final score from three sources. First, total the victory points printed on all your claimed point cards. Second, add three points for each gold coin and one point for each silver coin you collected. Third, count your leftover spice cubes. Each non-yellow cube is worth one victory point. Yellow cubes are worth nothing. The player with the highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most spice cubes remaining wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with four players. Each player will take different actions so you can see how the game flows. Player 1 goes first, then Player 2, Player 3, and Player 4 clockwise. Follow along even if your table has a different number of players.

walkthrough.title: Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Let's check what everyone has. Each player starts with the same two merchant cards in hand: a production card that makes two yellow cubes and an upgrade card that upgrades two cubes. Player 1 has three yellow cubes, Player 2 has four yellow, Player 3 has four yellow, and Player 4 has three yellow plus one red. The merchant market has six cards and the point card market has five cards with coins on the first two.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Play Production Card
walkthrough.text: Player 1, let's start simple. Play your production card that generates two yellow cubes. Take two yellow cubes from the supply and add them to your caravan. You now have five yellow cubes total. Your production card stays face up on the table until you rest.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Acquire a Merchant Card
walkthrough.text: The leftmost card is free, but the third card costs two cubes because you skip two cards. Player 2 places one yellow cube on the first card and one yellow cube on the second card, then takes the third card into their hand. Player 2 now has two yellow cubes left and three merchant cards in hand.

walkthrough.title: Why Acquiring Deep Cards Can Pay Off
walkthrough.text: Spending cubes to acquire deeper cards is an investment. That trade card might let Player 2 convert spices efficiently for the rest of the game. Meanwhile, the cubes left on skipped cards are now bonuses for whoever picks up those cards next. Acquiring the right merchant card early can define your whole engine.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Play Production Card
walkthrough.text: That brings them to six yellow cubes total. A solid start, building up resources before deciding what to do with them.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Hoarding Yellow Cubes
walkthrough.text: Player 4 plays their production card to gain two yellow cubes, bringing their total to five yellow and one red, which is six cubes. Not bad yet. But here is the problem: Player 4 plans to keep producing yellow cubes next turn too, without upgrading or trading any of them. Yellow cubes are the least valuable spice, and the caravan limit is ten cubes. Filling up on yellow is a trap.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Upgrade Early, Don't Hoard Yellow
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is simple. Yellow cubes are easy to produce but worth nothing on their own. Point cards require higher-value spices, so you need to upgrade or trade. If you keep producing yellow without converting, you will hit the ten-cube cap with a caravan full of the cheapest spice. Use your upgrade card early to start moving cubes up the ladder.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Play Upgrade Card
walkthrough.text: They upgrade one yellow cube to red and then upgrade that same red cube to green. Now Player 1 has three yellow cubes and one green cube. That green cube is already halfway to meeting some point card requirements. Smart play, converting early.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Play the New Trade Card
walkthrough.text: This card converts two yellow cubes into one red cube, and it is repeatable. Player 2 has two yellow cubes, so they run the trade once and get one red cube. Player 2 now has one red cube and one merchant card left in hand. Their engine is starting to take shape.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Acquire a Free Merchant Card
walkthrough.text: Remember, the leftmost position costs nothing. This card also has a yellow cube sitting on it from Player 2's earlier acquisition, so Player 3 picks that up too. Free card plus a bonus cube. Always check what is sitting on cards before you decide which to take.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Resting Too Early
walkthrough.text: Player 4 has played only one card so far, their production card. Instead of playing their upgrade card or acquiring a new merchant card, Player 4 decides to rest and pick up the production card. That means Player 4 spent an entire turn just to recover one card. Meanwhile, every other player is building their engine or converting spices.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Rest Until You Must
walkthrough.text: Resting is necessary, but it costs you a full turn. You want to play as many cards as possible before resting. Ideally, play all your merchant cards, then rest to pick them all up at once. Resting after playing just one card is a huge tempo loss. Think of rest as reloading a gun. You want to fire every shot before you reload.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Rest and Reload
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has now played both of their starting merchant cards. They take the rest action and pick up both cards. This is the right time to rest because there are no cards left to play. Player 1 reloads with a full hand and is ready to go again next turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Claim a Point Card
walkthrough.text: Wait, Player 2 only has one red cube. That is not enough. So instead, Player 2 looks for a cheaper card further down the row. They find one requiring one red and two yellow cubes. They pay those exact cubes and claim the card. No coin on this one since it was not in the first or second position, but it is still points on the board.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Play Upgrade Card
walkthrough.text: They upgrade two yellow cubes to red. Now Player 3 has two red cubes and three yellow cubes. With the trade card they acquired earlier, they are setting up a nice conversion pipeline.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Ignoring the Coin Race
walkthrough.text: Player 4 has enough spices to claim the second point card in the market, which has a silver coin on it worth one bonus victory point. But Player 4 decides to wait and save up for a higher-value card deeper in the row instead. On Player 1's next turn, Player 1 claims that second card and takes the silver coin. Player 4 just lost a free victory point.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Ignore the Coins
walkthrough.text: Gold and silver coins are bonus victory points that go to whoever claims the card first. Three extra points from a gold coin can easily decide the game. If you can afford a card with a coin on it, seriously consider grabbing it even if it is not the highest-value card available. Speed matters in Century: Spice Road. The coin race rewards players who claim cards quickly rather than waiting for the perfect opportunity.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, let's cover the biggest beginner traps. First, hoarding yellow cubes without upgrading them. Yellow is worth nothing at end of game and cannot buy good point cards. Second, resting too frequently. Every rest is a wasted turn where opponents are advancing. Third, ignoring the coin race. Gold coins are worth three victory points and can easily swing the game. Fourth, acquiring too many merchant cards without ever claiming point cards. Building a perfect engine means nothing if someone else ends the game before you score. Keep these in mind from the start.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Your Merchant Card Acquisitions
walkthrough.text: Not all merchant cards are created equal. The best cards for your engine are the ones that efficiently convert low-value spices into high-value ones. Trade cards that convert two yellow into one green, or one red into one brown, are extremely powerful. Production cards that generate red or green cubes directly are also strong because they skip the upgrade step entirely. When browsing the market, think about which card fills a gap in your conversion pipeline rather than just grabbing the cheapest option.

walkthrough.title: Build a Tight Engine
walkthrough.text: The strongest strategy in Century: Spice Road is to build a small, focused engine. Three to five merchant cards that work together will beat a hand of eight random ones. You want a production card to generate spices, a trade or upgrade card to convert them, and then the ability to claim a point card every two or three turns. Avoid collecting merchant cards for the sake of having options. Each extra card you own is another turn spent resting when you could be scoring.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Rest Timing
walkthrough.text: Resting is the most important tempo decision in the game. Every card you play before resting is value. Every rest is a dead turn. The ideal rhythm is to play all your merchant cards in sequence, claim a point card, then rest. If you have four merchant cards, you should play all four, score, then rest. That is six turns to score one point card. A player with three efficient cards can play all three, score, rest, and do it again in five turns. Fewer, better cards often beat more cards because the rest cycles are shorter.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Point Card Market
walkthrough.text: Always be aware of what point cards are available and what your opponents might be building toward. If you see a high-value card that requires brown and green cubes, and your opponent is stockpiling green, they are probably going for it. Either race to claim it first or focus on a different card that suits your spices. Also pay attention to the cards sliding into the first and second positions. Those are about to get coins, and coins change the value calculation significantly.

walkthrough.title: The Coin Race Matters
walkthrough.text: Gold and silver coins are often the difference between winning and losing. Three bonus victory points from a gold coin is enormous in a game where point cards typically range from six to twenty points. Sometimes it is better to claim a lower-value card with a gold coin than a higher-value card without one. For example, claiming a ten-point card with a gold coin gives you thirteen points, while that fifteen-point card without a coin is still just fifteen. Always factor coins into your scoring math.

walkthrough.title: Pace the End Game
walkthrough.text: The end trigger is five point cards in a four or five player game, or six in a two or three player game. You need to be aware of everyone's point card count. If you see someone with four cards, the game could end this round. Don't get caught mid-engine-build when the game ends. At the same time, if you are ahead, consider rushing the end by claiming cards quickly, even slightly lower-value ones. Ending the game before opponents can catch up is a valid winning strategy.

walkthrough.title: Leftover Spice Scoring
walkthrough.text: At the end of the game, each non-yellow spice cube in your caravan is worth one victory point. This means red, green, and brown cubes all score equally as leftovers. While you should not plan your strategy around leftover points, it is worth remembering when making your final decisions. If the game is about to end and you cannot claim another point card, spend your last turn upgrading cubes rather than producing more yellow. Those upgraded cubes will be worth one point each instead of zero.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't hoard yellow cubes — they're worth 0 VP and can't buy good point cards
Don't rest after playing just 1 card — play all cards before resting
Don't ignore coins — gold (3 VP) and silver (1 VP) swing games
Don't over-acquire merchant cards without scoring point cards

summary.title: Prioritize Your Merchant Card Acquisitions

Production cards generating red/green skip the upgrade step
Fill gaps in your conversion pipeline, don't grab randomly
Quality over quantity in merchant card selection

summary.title: Build a Tight Engine
bullets: 3-5 synergistic merchant cards beat 8 random ones
Ideal loop: produce → convert → claim → rest → repeat
Fewer cards = shorter rest cycles = faster scoring
Don't collect cards for optionality — each card adds rest overhead

summary.title: Manage Your Rest Timing
bullets: Play ALL merchant cards before resting
Ideal rhythm: play cards → claim point card → rest
Fewer efficient cards = tighter cycles = more point cards per game
Every rest is a dead turn — minimize them

summary.title: Watch the Point Card Market
bullets: Track what opponents are building toward
Race for contested cards or pivot to uncontested ones
Watch which cards slide into coin positions (1st and 2nd)
Coins change the value math significantly

summary.title: The Coin Race Matters
bullets: Gold coin (3 VP) can swing close games
10-point card + gold coin (13 VP) > 15-point card without coin
Always factor coins into your scoring math
Claiming fast for coins is often better than waiting for perfect cards

bullets: End trigger: 5th point card (4-5 players) or 6th (2-3 players)
Track opponents' point card counts — don't get caught mid-build
If ahead, rush the end with quick claims
If behind, delay by targeting high-value cards

summary.title: Leftover Spice Scoring
bullets: Non-yellow cubes = 1 VP each at end of game; yellow = 0 VP
If you can't claim another card, upgrade cubes on your last turn
Don't plan around leftovers, but don't waste them either

walkthrough.title: The Speed Strategy
walkthrough.text: One of the most effective strategies in Century: Spice Road is pure speed. Instead of building an elaborate engine, focus on claiming low-cost point cards as fast as possible. Look for cards requiring only yellow and red cubes. These are cheap and quick to fulfill. Pair a strong production card with a basic trade card and start claiming cards every three or four turns. You rack up coins by always being first to claim the leftmost cards, and you trigger the end game before slow builders can score their big cards.

walkthrough.title: Building the Speed Engine
walkthrough.text: For the speed strategy, keep your merchant card count low. Three cards is ideal. You want a production card that generates multiple yellow cubes, a trade card that converts yellow to red efficiently, and your starting upgrade card as a backup. Your cycle is produce, trade, claim, rest. Four turns per point card. With gold and silver coins adding three and one points respectively, those cheap six and eight point cards can total ten or eleven points each in the early game. Speed wins when opponents are still setting up.

walkthrough.title: The Big Card Strategy
walkthrough.text: The opposite approach targets the highest-value point cards, usually worth fifteen to twenty points. These cards require rare green and brown cubes, which means you need a powerful conversion engine. Acquire merchant cards that jump multiple steps on the spice ladder, like converting yellow directly to green, or trading two red for one brown. You will claim fewer cards total, but each one is worth significantly more. The risk is that speed players may end the game before you score enough.

walkthrough.title: Building the Big Card Engine
walkthrough.text: For the big card strategy, invest your first three or four turns acquiring the right merchant cards. Look for production cards that generate red or green cubes directly, and trade cards that output brown cubes. You may need four or five merchant cards total, which means slightly longer rest cycles. The payoff is that when your engine fires, it produces the exact spices needed for twenty-point cards. Time your claims to grab gold coins whenever possible to offset the slower pace.

walkthrough.title: The Hybrid Approach
walkthrough.text: The strongest players adapt their strategy to what the market offers rather than committing to speed or big cards from the start. Watch the merchant card market. If efficient low-to-high converters appear early, lean toward big cards. If the market is full of production cards, lean toward speed. Also watch what other players are doing. If everyone is going slow, you can exploit the coin race with a speed pivot. If everyone is racing, there is less competition for the expensive point cards.

walkthrough.title: Acquisition Timing and Market Manipulation
walkthrough.text: The merchant card market is a shared resource, and the acquisition mechanic creates interesting dynamics. When you skip cards and place cubes on them, you are making those cards more attractive to opponents. Sometimes it is worth taking a card you do not need just to deny it to an opponent or to avoid loading up cards that benefit them. Conversely, if a perfect card appears deep in the row, calculate whether the cube cost is worth it. Two or three cubes spent acquiring a game-changing trade card is almost always a good deal.

walkthrough.title: Reading the End Game
walkthrough.text: As the game nears its end, your strategy should shift. Count everyone's point cards. If someone has four cards in a five-player game, you have at most a few turns left. Stop acquiring merchant cards and start converting everything you have into claims. Sometimes a suboptimal claim is better than no claim. Also consider upgrading leftover spices on your final turn if you cannot claim anything. Each non-yellow cube is worth one victory point, and converting five yellow cubes to five red cubes nets five free points.

walkthrough.title: Player Count Adjustments
walkthrough.text: Strategy shifts depending on player count. With two or three players, the game lasts longer because you need six point cards to trigger the end. This favors the big card strategy since you have more time to build your engine. With four or five players, only five cards trigger the end, and more players means more competition for coins. Speed becomes more valuable at higher player counts. Also, with more players, cards slide through the market faster, so good merchant cards may not survive a full round of turns.

summary.title: The Speed Strategy
bullets: Target low-cost point cards (yellow/red requirements)
Claim quickly to grab gold/silver coins
Trigger end game before slow builders can score
Speed wins when opponents are still setting up their engines

summary.title: Building the Speed Engine
bullets: Keep hand to ~3 merchant cards for tight cycles
Cycle: produce → trade → claim → rest (4 turns per point card)
Cheap 6-8 point cards + gold coin = 9-11 points each
Minimize rest turns by keeping card count low

summary.title: The Big Card Strategy
bullets: Target 15-20 point cards requiring green/brown cubes
Need strong conversion engine: yellow → green, red → brown
Fewer claims but much higher VP per card
Risk: speed players may end the game before you score enough

summary.title: Building the Big Card Engine
bullets: Invest first 3-4 turns acquiring the right merchant cards
Look for production cards generating red/green directly
4-5 merchant cards total, longer rest cycles
Grab gold coins to offset slower pace

summary.title: The Hybrid Approach
bullets: Adapt to what the market offers rather than pre-committing
Good converters available → lean big cards
Production-heavy market → lean speed
Read opponents: if all go slow, exploit the coin race

summary.title: Acquisition Timing and Market Manipulation
bullets: Placing cubes on skipped cards makes them better for opponents
Sometimes take a card to deny it to an opponent
2-3 cubes for a game-changing trade card is almost always worth it
Factor in bonus cubes sitting on cards when evaluating cost

summary.title: Reading the End Game
bullets: Count opponents' point cards — know when the game will end
Stop acquiring, start converting and claiming
Suboptimal claim > no claim when game is ending
Final turn: upgrade leftovers (non-yellow cubes = 1 VP each)

summary.title: Player Count Adjustments
bullets: 2-3 players (6-card trigger): favors big card strategy, more time to build
4-5 players (5-card trigger): favors speed, more coin competition
Higher player count = merchant cards cycle faster through market
Adjust strategy to match your table size

entries.definition: The most common and lowest-value spice cube. Yellow cubes are easy to produce but worth zero victory points as leftovers. Most production cards generate yellow cubes. The starting point of the spice ladder.
The second-tier spice cube. Red cubes are one step above yellow on the spice ladder. Many point cards require red cubes, and they are worth one victory point each as leftovers.
The third-tier spice cube. Green cubes are harder to produce and often required by medium- to high-value point cards. Worth one victory point each as leftovers.
The rarest and most valuable spice cube. Brown cubes are at the top of the spice ladder and required by the highest-value point cards. Worth one victory point each as leftovers.
The hierarchy of spice values from lowest to highest: yellow (turmeric) to red (saffron) to green (cardamom) to brown (cinnamon). Upgrade cards move cubes up this ladder one step at a time.
A player's personal supply of spice cubes. The caravan has a maximum capacity of ten cubes. If gaining cubes would exceed ten, the player must discard cubes of their choice down to ten.
The maximum of ten spice cubes a player can hold at any time. Excess cubes gained from production, trade, or acquisition bonuses must be discarded down to ten. Player's choice which cubes to discard.
A type of merchant card that generates spice cubes from the supply. Shows cube icons with no arrows. When played, take the shown cubes and add them to your caravan. Not repeatable.

A type of merchant card that converts one set of spice cubes into another. Shows input cubes, an arrow, and output cubes. When played, pay the input and take the output. Repeatable: you can execute the conversion multiple times in a single play.

A type of merchant card that moves cubes up the spice ladder. Each upgrade moves one cube up exactly one step (yellow to red, red to green, or green to brown). The number of upgrades varies by card. You can stack upgrades on the same cube or spread them.

One of the four possible actions on your turn. Place a merchant card from your hand face up in front of you and resolve its effect (production, trade, or upgrade). The card remains played until you rest.

One of the four possible actions on your turn. Take a merchant card from the market row into your hand. The leftmost card is free. Each card further right costs one spice cube placed on each skipped card. Collect any bonus cubes sitting on the acquired card.

One of the four possible actions on your turn. Pay the exact spice combination shown on a face-up point card, return those cubes to the supply, and take the card into your scoring pile. Also take any coin on the card.

One of the four possible actions on your turn. Pick up all previously played merchant cards and return them to your hand. No other action is taken this turn. You cannot rest if you have no played cards.

Cards that score victory points. Each shows a spice cost that must be paid exactly and a victory point value. Five are displayed face-up in the market row at all times. When one is claimed, cards slide left and a new one is dealt to the right.
A row of six face-up merchant cards available for acquisition. The leftmost card is free. When a card is taken, remaining cards slide left and a new card is dealt to the rightmost position.

A row of five face-up point cards available for claiming. Gold coins sit on the first card and silver coins on the second. When a card is claimed, remaining cards slide left, a new card is dealt right, and coins are placed on the new first and second positions.

A bonus token placed on the leftmost point card in the market. Worth three victory points at end of game. Taken by whoever claims that point card. Replaced on the new leftmost card when the market shifts.
A bonus token placed on the second point card in the market. Worth one victory point at end of game. Taken by whoever claims that point card. Replaced on the new second card when the market shifts.

The game end is triggered when any player claims their fifth point card (four or five players) or sixth point card (two or three players). The current round is completed so all players have equal turns before final scoring.

Total victory points from three sources: point card values, coin bonuses (gold three points each, silver one point each), and leftover non-yellow spice cubes (one point each). Yellow cubes are worth zero. Tiebreaker: most spice cubes remaining.

A property unique to trade cards. When playing a trade card, you may execute the conversion shown as many times as you want, as long as you can pay the input each time. Production and upgrade cards are not repeatable.
